trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 2 --batch_size 256 --num_workers 4 --emb_dim 256 --num_layers 5 --dropout 0.2
trajectory.description: Observation: fresh workspace with no code; task is ogbg-molpcba graph multi-label classification with severe imbalance and mandatory early eval. Analysis: need a reliable PyG baseline quickly, with offline-safe predict.py and short initial training to validate pipeline and measure runtime. Decision: implement a compact GIN+virtual-node style model with learned categorical atom/bond embeddings, masked BCE loss with per-task pos_weight, average precision validation, and run 2 epochs before immediate eval.

Observation: initial run crashed with exit 139 after ~351s before eval, likely due to memory pressure from loading full train/val .pt into Python lists plus multiworker DataLoader/pin_memory on a very large dataset. Analysis: the code currently torch.loads entire train and val objects and keeps all lists in RAM; with 350k graphs this can exceed memory and segfault. We need a lighter first-pass baseline that still satisfies early-eval requirement. Decision: switch to OGB's built-in PCBA dataset loader (already installed, processed locally), use official split indices, smaller model/batch, safer num_workers=0, and robust predict.py using the same dataset format. Then run 2 epochs and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 2 --batch_size 128 --emb_dim 192 --num_layers 5 --dropout 0.2 --train_limit 50000 --val_limit 10000 --num_workers 0
trajectory.description: Observation: current workspace still contains the original RAM-heavy train.py and predict.py despite the prior plan to switch to OGB loader; first run crashed from loading full serialized lists. Analysis: we need to actually rewrite code to use ogbg-molpcba processed dataset with index splits, safer loaders, and a local predict.py that reads test.pt. To satisfy the mandatory early eval, run a short 2-epoch training on a manageable subset and submit immediately. Decision: update train.py to use PygGraphPropPredDataset for train/valid, keep model.py mostly intact, harden predict.py with num_workers=0, then train briefly and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 2 --train_limit 12000 --val_limit 4000 --batch_size 64 --num_workers 0 && python predict.py --data_path /data --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output pred_check.json
trajectory.error_summary: bash: line 1:   192 Segmentation fault      (core dumped) python train.py --epochs 2 --train_limit 12000 --val_limit 4000 --batch_size 64 --num_workers 0
trajectory.description: Observation: training still crashed immediately with exit 139, before any Python traceback, after switching to OGB loader. Analysis: this strongly suggests a low-level segfault in the OGB/PyG dataset path or indexing, not ordinary Python OOM. The safest route is to avoid OGB dataset internals entirely and use the provided train.pt/val.pt files, but in a memory-safe way: lazy per-graph access via mmap torch.load, no full materialization, no workers, and a tiny first baseline. Decision: rewrite train.py around a lazy serialized-list dataset using the provided .pt files, simplify model if needed, run a very short subset training, locally test predict.py, then submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: both OGB loader and direct torch.load(train.pt) crash with immediate exit 139, indicating a low-level deserialization issue with the provided giant .pt files in this environment rather than ordinary Python memory pressure. Analysis: we need to isolate whether the crash happens on torch.load itself and find a safer data source. Since ogb and rdkit are preinstalled, the most robust path is to use the local OGB processed dataset files directly via PygGraphPropPredDataset root=/data, but first verify minimal imports and dataset construction in tiny diagnostic scripts. Also, current model code uses in-place clamp_ on tensor views, which is risky though unlikely to cause startup segfault; we can clean that up. Decision: add a tiny diagnose.py to test torch.load and OGB dataset creation separately, simplify model clamps, and run diagnostics before another training attempt.

trajectory.description: Observation: minimal Python/torch environment works, but both torch.load on provided giant .pt files and OGB dataset construction trigger immediate segfaults, suggesting a low-level issue with those serialized graph sources in this environment. Analysis: we need an alternate local data source. Since the task is ogbg-molpcba and rdkit is installed, the raw CSV.gz and split files may exist under /data even if processed loaders crash. A robust fallback is to build our own dataset from raw SMILES using RDKit, avoiding torch.load/OGB internals entirely. First we must inspect /data recursively for raw files and verify RDKit can parse them. Decision: add a filesystem/raw-data diagnostic script and run it; if raw molpcba files exist, next iteration will implement a custom RDKit->PyG pipeline and resume the mandatory early baseline/eval.

trajectory.command: python diagnose.py /data
trajectory.description: Observation: recursive filesystem scan shows /data only contains train.pt and val.pt at visible depths; no raw CSV fallback is available. Analysis: both torch.load and OGB dataset creation segfault in this environment, so standard training/prediction on provided graph files is blocked. We still need to make progress toward the mandatory eval and isolate whether an alternate deserialization path exists. PyTorch 2.11 supports weights_only and mmap options; sometimes legacy pickle paths crash while mmap/weights_only or a fresh subprocess with faulthandler can reveal more. Also, predict.py currently depends on torch.load(test.pt), which would likely fail in eval too unless we find a safe load mode. Decision: add deeper diagnostics to try multiple torch.load modes on train/val in isolated subprocesses and inspect file headers; if any mode works, pivot code accordingly next iteration. If all fail, we may need to stop because the dataset is unusable in this container.
